Although 10-year US Treasuries may be holding close to 4½% yields, there is a compelling case that they should be already much higher. It may even explain why the US Fed seems less willing to cut policy rates? Evidence how despite last month’s widespread fears over a trade-induced US recession, the yield on the 10-year note (orange line) has stayed resolutely above its 200-day moving average. See chart. comment.body:
🔼 IF Above 5950

→ 5970–5985
• Gamma shelf = first resistance band
• May fade unless vol picks up

→ IF 5985 Reclaimed → THEN 6000–6020
• Speed pocket opens up
• Thin zone → breakout possible, but needs vol support

→ IF 6020 Clears → THEN 6050–6060
• Top-end shelf
• Higher gamma = stall risk unless driven by catalyst

🔽 IF Below 5950

→ 5925–5905
• Pullback support
• Possible drift/pin region

→ IF 5905 Fails → THEN 5870–5850
• Fast drop zone
• Less structural support → vulnerable to vol spike

→ IF 5850 Breaks → THEN 5820–5835
• Thickest lower gamma shelf
• Expect defensive flows and potential bounce

🔄 Chop Zone: 5925–5985

• Two-way price action
• Vol expansion needed for a break

🧭 TAKEAWAY

• 5950–5970 = resistance test
• 5985+ = breakout zone
• 5925–5905 = drift support
• Below 5905 = caution into 5850
